coredata.dc:title: A hydrothermal-carbonization process for simultaneously production of sugars, graphene quantum dots, and porous carbon from sugarcane bagasse
coredata.dc:creator: Chai, Xinyu
He, Hui
Fan, Hanhan
Kang, Xiheng
Song, Xueping
coredata.dc:description: A green and facile approach was proposed to simultaneously produce fermentative sugar (FS), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and hierarchical porous carbon (HPC) from sugarcane bagasse through the hydrothermal-carbonization process. In this work, the maximum yields of FS were 35.77%, 30.54%, 1.23%, 28.52%, and 41.85% for xylose, mannose, glucose, galactose, and arabinose, respectively. The GQDs, with bright blue fluorescence, had an average diameter at 2.26 nm, and exhibited a well-defined spherical shape and graphene structure. The formation mechanism of GQDs was further investigated, and the GQDs mainly derived from the dissolved lignin and polysaccharides. Moreover, the HPC presented a much higher surface area and controllable oxygen content than non-hydrothermal pretreatment porous carbon, whose unique pore structure was mainly resulted from the dissolution of FS. The green and facile approach provides a novel pathway to produce high value-added materials from sugarcane bagasse, developing a foundation for the preparation of better biomass materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sugarcane bagasse
Hot-water pretreatment
Fermentative sugar
Graphene quantum dots
Porous carbon

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The optical-chemical properties and formation mechanism of GQDs
The physicochemical properties of porous carbon

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The yields of sugar and solid residue under different conditions: (a) liquid-solid ratio, (b) holding time, and (c) maximum temperature.
The nanoparticle diameter histogram of GQDs.
(a) The adsorption-desorption isotherm and (b, c) the pore size distribution of samples.
The BET surface area and pore volume of PC and HPC.
The EA and XPS results of porous carbon.

head.ce:title: A hydrothermal-carbonization process for simultaneously production of sugars, graphene quantum dots, and porous carbon from sugarcane bagasse

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, Nanning 530004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green and facile approach was proposed to simultaneously produce fermentative sugar (FS), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and hierarchical porous carbon (HPC) from sugarcane bagasse through the hydrothermal-carbonization process. In this work, the maximum yields of FS were 35.77%, 30.54%, 1.23%, 28.52%, and 41.85% for xylose, mannose, glucose, galactose, and arabinose, respectively. The GQDs, with bright blue fluorescence, had an average diameter at 2.26 nm, and exhibited a well-defined spherical shape and graphene structure. The formation mechanism of GQDs was further investigated, and the GQDs mainly derived from the dissolved lignin and polysaccharides. Moreover, the HPC presented a much higher surface area and controllable oxygen content than non-hydrothermal pretreatment porous carbon, whose unique pore structure was mainly resulted from the dissolution of FS. The green and facile approach provides a novel pathway to produce high value-added materials from sugarcane bagasse, developing a foundation for the preparation of better biomass materials.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of a new biomass material industry to solve problems derived from the energy crisis and environmental pollution is a common goal throughout the world. Researchers have taken considerable effort to produce high value-added materials through the bio-refinery process (Chen et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2018; Liang et al., 2014; Ramanan et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2016). The pretreatment step can potentially separate different components from lignocellulose material, to a certain extent, which causes an increase in the surface area for post-treatment through partially breaking the physical barrier of plants (Batista et al., 2018). Several pretreatment techniques have been reported, including hot-water pretreatment (HWP) (Song et al., 2011), alkali extraction (Song et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2019), and organic solvent pretreatment (Zhang et al., 2016). Among them, HWP has greatly caught the attention of researchers because of its environmentally friendly status, low operating cost, and low corrosive advantages (Batista et al., 2018). In the HWP process, the selective degradation of hemicellulose was catalyzed by hydronium ion, as both cellulose and lignin fractions were obtained in minor amounts (Song et al., 2016). Moreover, researchers have paid little attention to the trace dissolved lignin obtained in the HWP process, ignoring its potential applications.

As one of the most productive crop wastes in the world, sugarcane bagasse has the advantages of large output, concentrated producing area, low cost, and stable and uniform properties, which are beneficial for industrial production. The structure of sugarcane bagasse is dominated by lignocellulose, with 38–59% cellulose, 18–26% hemicellulose, and 16–25% lignin (Batista et al., 2018). Compared with other biomass materials, such as wood, sugarcane bagasse contains more hemicellulose, and its structure is loose. This structural feature of sugarcane bagasse makes it easy to extract fermentative sugar (FS) for the preparation of industrial ethanol (Khatiwada et al., 2016). On the other hand, sugarcane bagasse contains abundant carbon and oxygen elements, and lower ash content than other agricultural wastes (Arshad and Ahmed, 2016), giving it great potential for use in the preparation of carbon materials. However, the use of sugarcane bagasse, which is usually burned directly as fuel or used in the preparation of bio-fertilizer (Alves et al., 2015), for energy and production purposes is still in the early stages. The method of high-valued sugarcane bagasse utilization still needs to be explored.

Carbon material is a broad category, including functional materials such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (Zhang et al., 2018b), carbon nanotubes (Khoerunnisa et al., 2019), graphene (Yoo et al., 2019), activated carbon (Ren et al., 2017), and activated carbon fibers (Buchmeiser et al., 2019). GQDs were first discovered in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004), showing many advantages as compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots, including high photo-stability, high aqueous solubility, robust chemical inertness, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and easy modification. With these advantages, GQDs have attracted much attention in recent years (Gao et al., 2019). Moreover, various applications have been explored for GQDs, including drug delivery (Wang et al., 2018), bio-imaging (Chen et al., 2018; Qiao et al., 2018), fluorescent probing and analysis (Pacquiao et al., 2018), and heavy metal indicators (Liu et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018a). In addition, various raw materials have been used to prepare it, involving vegetables (Wang et al., 2014a), chitosan (Liang et al., 2016), sodium citrate (Liu et al., 2017), and polymers (Ramanan et al., 2018). Among these raw materials, biomass material has occupied an important position because of its high carbon and rich heteroatom content. However, many current studies have not carried out in-depth studies on the structure and composition of biomass quantum dots, and its formation mechanism, which has affected the investigation of the preparation of GQDs.

Contrary to GQDs, porous carbon has been investigated for many years. As an excellent adsorbent, it possesses a very large specific surface area, high pore volume, and multiple active sites for doping, which have broad applications in pollutant adsorption (Zhu et al., 2017), super capacitors (Chen et al., 2017), hydrogen storage (Wang et al., 2016), and catalysis (Liang et al., 2017). By controlling three key properties of activated carbon, namely, the specific surface area, pore structure, and surface chemical functional groups, it can be adapted to the requirements of different applications. Generally, the surface properties of activated carbon are controlled under harsh chemical treatment. For example, researchers (Jahandar Lashaki et al., 2016) have introduced oxygen groups into activated carbon by nitric acid treatment. On the other hand, the removal of oxygen-containing functional groups often requires high temperatures (Hashisho et al., 2009). Therefore, developing eco-friendly alternative methods still needs to be explored. Considering that sugarcane bagasse is rich in hemicellulose, the process of pre-extracting hemicellulose actually removes different amounts of oxygen and carbon elements. Therefore, under the same preparation method, some special activated carbons with different hydrophilicities and hydrophobicities would be expected to be produced with the sugarcane bagasse, containing different combinations of oxygen and carbon elements as a result of different HWP.

However, so far there are few reports on the simultaneous preparation of GQDs, FS, and porous carbon using biomass raw materials. Xu et al. (2016) had simultaneously prepared carbon nanodots and activated carbon using soybeans, but the formation mechanism of these products has rarely been investigated. In this work, a green process was reported to prepare FS and GQDs, derived from extract liquid, and to simultaneously produce controllable porous carbon from solid residue extract through the hydrothermal-carbonization process. Moreover, both the effects of hydrothermal pretreatment on the physicochemical properties of porous carbon and the formation mechanism were deeply investigated in this work. This research will greatly improve the comprehensive utilization value of sugarcane bagasse and provide a theoretical basis for obtaining more valuable products.

Sugarcane bagasse was obtained from a sugarcane factory, and it was air-dried and smashed to 200 mesh. Phosphoric acid (H3PO4 > 85.0%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Sci-Tech Co., Ltd. (American). During the entire experimental process, neutral water was provided by the Merck millipore water purification system.

First, the smashed sugarcane bagasse was pretreated through the HWP process, and then, 3.0 g of smashed sugarcane bagasse was put into a Teflon-lined autoclave with different liquid–solid mass ratios (4:1, 5:1, 6:1, 7:1, 8:1). Then, the sugarcane bagasse was heated to a maximum temperature (140 °C, 150 °C, 160 °C, 170 °C, 180 °C) at a constant rate of 1.7 °C /min, and were kept at different holding times (0 h, 1 h, 2 h, 3 h, 4 h) at the maximum temperature. When the HWP process was completed, the reaction was stopped immediately by cooling in an ice-water bath. The obtained product was diluted with 50-mL neutral water, and the supernatant and residual solid were separated and collected after 15-min ultrasonic treatment.

The supernatant samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the coarse particles, and then, the samples were dialyzed against neutral water, using dialysis membrane (MWCO of 500) for 3 days. After dialysis, the solution was collected for further investigation of GQDs.

Next, the solid residue was denoted as SR-T, where SR represents solid residue sugarcane bagasse, and T indicates the maximum temperature in the HWP process. The SR was dried to a constant weight at 105 °C. After drying, the SR was impregnated into 50% w/w phosphoric acid for 12 h with an impregnated ratio of 1:3. Then, the samples were carbonized for 2 h in a tube oven at 500 °C at the heating rate of 10 °C /min. The N2 was used as a protecting gas during the carbonization process at a flow rate of 150 mL/min. After carbonization, the porous carbon was washed with neutral water at 60 °C and then was dried to a constant weight in a vacuum oven for further investigation. The dried samples, which underwent hot-water pretreatment, were denoted as HPC-T, where T indicates the maximum temperature in the HWP process. A controlled sample was prepared in the same method with non-thermal pretreatment, and it was denoted as PC.

The sugar in the extract liquid was first derivatized through two-step acid hydrolysis. High-performance liquid chromatography (e2595, Waters, American) was used to detect the content of polysaccharide. The chromatographic column was SB-C 18 (Agilent, American), and the column temperature was 30 °C. Acetonitrile and 0.2 mol/L of ammonium acetate liquor were used as a mobile phase. The ratio of acetonitrile and ammonium acetate liquor was 18:83, and the liquor flow rate was 1 mL/min. All experiment data were tested three times.

The fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to analyze the fluorescence properties of GQDs, and the optical absorption spectrum was measured by a UV-8453 (Agilent, Japan) spectrometer. The morphology characterization of GQDs was observed by TECNAI G2 F30 high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) (FEI, USA) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Dimension Edge, Bruker, Germany). The functional groups of GQDs were detected through a TENSOR II FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). In addition, the elemental composition was obtained by a Perkin-Elmer 2400II CHONS analyzer system (PE, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected using an ESCALAB 250XI+ (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with Al-Kα radiation.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (SU8220, Hitachi, Japan) was used to observe the surface morphology of porous carbon. The sample conductivity was improved by coating a thin golden layer. The HR-TEM images were also obtained by a FEI TECNAI G2 F30. The surface chemistry of the sample was characterized by elemental analysis (EA) and XPS. The elemental composition was obtained by a Perkin-Elmer 2400II CHONS analyzer system (PE, USA). XPS data were collected using an ESCALAB 250XI+ (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with Al-Kα radiation.

Glucose, xylose, mannose, galactose, and arabinose are the major components of the extract liquid. Xylose, mannose, galactose, and arabinose are mainly derived from hemicellulose degradation, and glucose is obtained from dissolved cellulose. Similar conclusions have been reported in other studies (Batista et al., 2018). The yields of the sugars and solid residue in the HWP process under different conditions are shown in Fig. 1 . Results revealed that FS content slightly increased for all components along with the enhancement of the liquid-solid ratio, but the solid residue yield decreased from 84.84% to 77.85%. When the maximum temperature increased from 140 °C to 180 °C, the FS yields achieved maximum values at 170 °C. When the reaction temperature was increased to over 170 °C, the main reason for the sharp drop in sugar yield was that part of sugar was simultaneously transformed to furfural and other small molecules. In addition, a solid residue yield of 78.70% was obtained at 170 °C. The effects of holding time on the yields of sugar at the maximum temperature were also investigated. Results indicated that the sugar yields for all components increased with holding time, and the solid residue yield decreased from 96.10% to 75.10%.

Since the solid residue yield would directly affect the subsequent activated carbon yield, to obtain better fermentable sugar and residue yield, the optimal pretreatment conditions were 1:5, 170 °C, and 3 h for the liquid-solid ratio, maximum temperature, and holding time, respectively. Under these conditions, a solid residue yield of 79.25% was achieved, and the sugar yields were 1.23%, 35.77%, 30.54%, 28.52%, and 41.85% for glucose, xylose, mannose, galactose, and arabinose, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical-chemical properties and formation mechanism of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The GQD aqueous solution was faint yellow in the sunlight and bright blue in UV irradiation at 365 nm. The GQDs, measured by UV–vis, had an absorption peak at 280 nm and a weak shoulder peak near 320 nm, which was associated with the π-π* transitions of the CC bond and the n-π* transition of CO, respectively. The fluorescence property was analyzed by FL spectrophotometer. The Maximum emission of GQDs was achieved at 461 nm, when the excitation wavelength was 390 nm. Moreover, the “giant red-edge effect” phenomenon was observed, which meant that the emission wavelength was heavily dependent on the excitation wavelength. The results were related to the graphene structure and abundant oxygen content of GQDs (Cushing et al., 2014). In other words, GQDs had superior and stable optical properties, which would provide good application prospects in sensors, biological imaging, and other fields.

The morphology of GQDs, observed through HR-TEM and AFM. At low magnification, the GQDs are equally dispersed in aqueous solution, which suggests that the GQDs had good water solubility. At high magnification, the GQDs are monodispersed and exhibit a well-defined spherical shape, while the hexagon structure of graphene was also observed. A clear lattice structure was observed by HR-TEM, and the lattice space was 0.24 nm, which corresponded to the (1120) lattice fringes of graphitic carbon (Iannazzo et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 2 , the average diameter was calculated to be 2.26 nm, based on HR-TEM, by measuring 100 random nanoparticles, and the diameter of GQDs was far smaller than that reported in other studies (Iannazzo et al., 2017). The height of GQDs was about 5 nm, according to the two-dimensional AFM images. The three-dimensional images of GQDs had also further indicated that the surface roughness was less than 5 nm. In addition, GQDs with ultra-small size could stably disperse in aqueous solution for at least half a year in room temperature, and it always exhibited the above characterizations after 6 months.

The EA and XPS results of GQDs indicated that GQDs were rich in oxygen and carbon, and bits of nitrogen and oxygen elements were also present. The O/C ratio was 1.05, which suggested that GQDs were hydrophilic (Ramanan et al., 2018). To further interpret the composition of surface functional groups of GQDs, peak deconvolutions were performed. In the XPS high-resolution spectra of C1s, the first bond was located at 284.6 eV, which could be associated with sp2 hybridized carbon, and the hybridized carbon was consistent with the graphene structure, as observed in the HR-TEM. Moreover, the bands at 285.2 eV, 286.3 eV, and 288.0 eV were attributed to C–C or C–O, carbonyl, and carboxyl, respectively. The peaks at 531.3 eV, 532.3 eV, and 533.2 eV in the high-resolution spectra of O1s were attributed to CO, OCOH, and CO, respectively. The results showed that some main groups such as carbonyl and carboxyl were present in sugarcane bagasse GQDs. In addition, nitrogen might only exist in the form of CN at 399.6 eV.

The FT-IR results could reveal more about the functional groups of GQDs. The appearance of a broad peak at 3435 cm−1 associated with the OH stretch indicates that the GQDs contained abundant oxygen groups. The sharp peak at 1609 cm−1 was presumed to indicate the presence of well-conjugated and/or aromatic CC merged with CO. The existence of the sp2 hybridized conjugated carbon had also been inferred by the results of XPS and HR-TEM, and the same proposal was proposed by Vadivel Ramanan et al (Ramanan et al., 2018). In addition, characteristic absorption peaks near 700–900 cm−1, were attributed to the aromatic sp2 bend, which is related to aromatic hydrogen in the lignin structure. The COC stretch, located at 1245 cm−1, was associated with the aryl-alkyl ether linkage. Moreover, the signal at 2946 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibrations of methyl/methylene groups in GQDs, and the corresponding bending vibrations on the aromatic ring were also observed at 1347 cm−1 (symmetrical) and 1438 cm−1 (asymmetrical). Thus, the acquisition of these aromatic groups was mainly due to the cleavage of lignin macromolecules. Additionally, the peak observed at 1119 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of C–O, and a weak signal at 1045 cm−1 was attributed to the absorption of C–N, which was consistent with the EA and XPS results.

Simultaneously, a weak characteristic absorption at 1740 cm−1 was related to the stretch of CO, which inferred the presence of hemicellulose (Gray et al., 2009). The results suggested that some of the polysaccharide macromolecules might have adhered to the outer layers of GQDs. Therefore, the above results showed that sp2 hybridized conjugated carbon is the core of GQDs, and abundant oxygen groups are located at the edge of carbon layers, including carbonyl functional groups, carboxyl groups, bits of pyrrolic nitrogen, and methyl/methylene.

The microfiber structure with three layers of fiber is very exquisite. Many reports have mentioned that lignin is the first layer, acting as a shell, and glycan is a binder between lignin and protofibril. Moreover, both lignin and glycan are present in the form of a lignin-carbohydrate complex (LCC), but glycan and protofibril are bound by hydrogen. The microfiber morphology of sugarcane bagasse and SR would reveal the effect of HWP on the formation mechanism of FS and GQDs. The edge of the sugarcane bagasse microfiber was integrated and smooth. After the sugarcane bagasse was pre-extracted at 140 °C, some holes were observed on the fiber surface, and the mass of an internally amorphous substance flowed out from the holes. Heating would be blamed for this phenomenon in a hydrothermal process. During this process, the hydronium ion eroded the fiber and formed some holes, eventually causing internal polysaccharides to erupt from the weak surface. Furthermore, by the time the temperature rose to 160 °C, the reaction had become intense, the integrated edge structure had become vague, and more small holes had appeared, suggesting a much greater dissolution of polysaccharides than that of SR-140. Moreover, the cracked substance from SR-160 dissolved to smaller fragments and particles, and some nanofibers were also observed and distributed around holes. The microfiber of SR-180 became hollow, and its edge was rougher than that of sugarcane bagasse microfiber. In addition, the internal structure of SR-180 microfiber was uneven, which was attributed to the dissolution of large amounts of glycan. Overall, with the increase in the heating temperature during the HWP process, many holes appeared on the surface of the microfiber, which provided many passageways for promoting the dissolution of FS.

Based on previous findings, trace amounts of cellulose and lignin were also dissolved in this process (Song et al., 2016). Through the abovementioned study, it was also found that the main core group of sugarcane bagasse quantum dots was graphene, with the existence of sp2 hybridized conjugated carbon. Moreover, since lignin contained an abundance of benzene ring structure, it provided the basis for the graphene structure of GQDs. Therefore, it can be concluded that the formation of sugarcane bagasse quantum dots was related to lignin and polysaccharides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The physicochemical properties of porous carbon
ce:section.ce:para: The porous structures of activated carbon were characterized through the BET analyzer, and the results were shown in Table 1 . Overall, the pore property of HPC was better than that of PC, and with the rise in hydrothermal temperature, the pore performance increased gradually. For example, the HPC-180 sample had the largest increase in surface area and micropore volume, and the total pore volume was, likewise, greatly improved, from 0.85 cm3/g to 1.28 cm3/g. Moreover, the HPC-160 sample improved, in the mesopore area, to a pore volume of 1.09 cm3/g. On the other hand, compared with that of PC, the porosity of HPC-140 was superior. For example, the BET surface area of HPC-140 increased from 824.19 m2/g to 960.53 m2/g, an increase of 16.5%, and the total pore volume increased from 0.85 cm3/g to 1.28 cm3/g. These results indicated that the hydrothermal pretreatment was beneficial to the improvement in pore performance of activated carbon.

The adsorption-desorption isotherm and pore size distribution of samples are shown in Fig. 3 . In Fig. 3(a), all samples exhibited a typical type-IV isotherm. In the entire adsorption process, the N2 adsorption quantity of all samples increased with relative pressure. At extremely low pressures, because of the presence of the micropore area, the adsorption quantity of all samples sharply increased. This result was consistent with other studies (Jain et al., 2014; Li et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2014b). Moreover, the existence of the mesopore area caused the emergence of hysteresis loops at high relative pressures in all samples (Kan et al., 2015; Puziy et al., 2005; Vazquez-Santos et al., 2012).

The pore size distribution would exhibit more precise information. As shown in Fig. 3(b) and (c), in all samples, an abundance of mesopores and micropores were present after carbonization. In the micropore area, the HPC had a much greater pore volume than PC had. In particular, both HPC-180 and HPC-160 possessed ultra-micropores (<1 nm), which enlarged the specific surface area to some extent. On the other hand, all samples exhibited mesopore areas that were more concentrated at 10–30 nm, particularly near 20 nm, than that of PC. In addition, the HPC-160 sample showed a much greater mesopore volume than HPC-140 or HPC-180 exhibited.

Since the surface morphology of different HPCs was similar, in this study, the HPC-180 sample served to reveal a different morphology than PC. The FE-SEM and TEM images of PC and HPC-180 are indicated that the surface morphology of HPC-180 was looser, and many macropores were exposed. The results were beneficial for mass transfer and facilitated the adsorption of some macromolecular gases. Moreover, smaller micropores were observed in HPC-180 than observed in PC, and some mesopores near 30 nm were also observed.

The surface chemical properties of porous carbon were characterized by EA and XPS, and the results are shown in Table 2 . The oxygen content of PC/HPC decreased with reaction temperature, from 31.45 to 17.81. The XPS survey showed similar results, with the oxygen content decreasing from 24.29 to 8.71. The O/C and H/C of porous carbon decreased with HWP temperature, which suggests the increase in aromatic groups in activated carbon (Zhang et al., 2017).

Only three major elements were observed in the wide-scan XPS spectrum, namely, C, O, and P. The existence of P was due to the residual elements in the phosphoric acid activation process. The high-resolution O1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into two sub-peaks, representing the carbonyl group (531.4 eV) and carboxyl group (533.2 eV). In general, the increase in hydrothermal temperature did not change the composition of oxygen groups on the carbon surface, but, as shown in Table 2, the oxygen content decreased radically. Moreover, the carboxyl group decreased much more than carbonyl decreased, and the ratio was reduced from 83.19% to 80.46%. Thus, the oxygen content of activated carbon could be adjusted by the change in the pre-treatment temperature.

The formation process of the unique pore diameter of HPC was investigated by TEM, without the HWP process, the pore diameter of PC was concentrated near 10 nm, which was consistent with the pore width distribution diagram of PC, as a result of phosphoric acid etching the surface. In addition, the TEM image of HPC-140 reveals the difficulty in maintaining the fiber shape. The etch of phosphoric acid broke it into small pieces, because the cavity formed by the hydrothermal process opened the internal channel for the impregnation of phosphoric acid, so that the phosphoric acid could fully contact the protofibril. Compared with that of HPC-140, the pore diameter of HPC-160 was mostly concentrated near 20 nm and presented an axial distribution, which might be related to the structural arrangement of the protofibril, and the HPC-180 exhibited an irregular shape, displaying the formation of a large number of mesopores.

Generally, the microfibril consists of three kinds of structures—lignin layer, glycan layer, and protofibril. The diameter of the protofibril is 3 nm, so the diameter of the microfiber is 24 to 30 nm. Based on the above conclusion, since the hydronium ion in the HWP process eroded the fiber, eventually, internal polysaccharides erupted from the weak surface. After most of the glycan was extracted, the microfibril structure became looser, and part of the interspace was formed between the protofibril and lignin layer. Afterwards, dried solid residue suffered from phosphoric acid impregnation, compared with non-hydrothermal pretreated sugarcane bagasse, as the phosphoric acid could contact a much greater fiber area. After the impregnation of phosphoric acid and carbonization, most of the impregnated fiber was etched to form pores and larger cavities. The pores were approximately 20–30 nm, which was consistent with the diameter of the microfibril. On the other hand, for the sugarcane bagasse fiber without hot-water pretreatment, most of the activator could not attack the interface with the fibers to form many mesopores. This conclusion was consistent with the pore size distribution of porous carbon in Fig. 3(b).

In this work, a novel process was proposed for obtaining FS, GQDs, and HPC, simultaneously. FS was dissolved from the hemicellulose in sugarcane bagasse fiber, while the GQDs were generated from lignin and polysaccharides in the extract liquid. HPC with different oxygen content was prepared by adjusting the temperature of the HWP process, and the unique pore diameter of HPC was obtained, on account of the glycan dissolution in the HWP process. This research has greatly improved the comprehensive utilization value of sugarcane bagasse, which lays a foundation for the preparation of better value-added biomass materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21766002), and the project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control (KF201608).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2019.02.126.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surplus electricity production in sugarcane mills using residual bagasse and straw as fuel
Energy

Green Chem.

Carbon
